title: America's $2M Secret: Why Its War Strategy Is Already Dead | Prof. Jiang Xueqin
full_text: So, here's a question that keeps coming  up in military circles, in think tanks, in government buildings, and honestly in  living rooms around the world right now. Can the United States actually win a modern  war using the strategy it has leaned on for the last 30 years? That strategy has a name. It's  called shock and awe. And if you've watched any coverage of American military operations over  the past few decades, you've heard that phrase. But do you actually know what it means? Do you  know where it came from? And more importantly, do you know why serious people are starting to  question whether it still works? Let's start from the beginning because this story goes back further  than most people realize. After World War II, the world was split between two massive  superpowers. The United States on one side, the Soviet Union on the other. Both countries  had nuclear weapons. Both countries had enormous armies. Both countries had the ability to destroy  each other completely. And that created one of the strangest situations in human history. You had  two of the most powerful nations ever to exist on this planet. And they could not fight each other  directly because if they did, both sides would be wiped out. Nuclear missiles would fly. Cities  would be destroyed. Millions of people would die within hours. And so you had this bizarre  balance where everyone was armed to the teeth, but nobody could actually pull the trigger. That  situation was called mutually assured destruction, which is a phrase that sounds almost darkly  comedic when you say it out loud. Mutually assured destruction, meaning if you shoot,  I shoot and we both die. That was the logic that kept the Cold War cold. And it forced the  American military to think differently. Instead of preparing for a direct confrontation with the  Soviets, the United States started focusing on something else entirely. They started focusing  on projecting power, on demonstrating strength, on creating the image that American military force  was so overwhelming, so technologically superior that any enemy would be foolish to challenge  it. And out of that thinking came the doctrine of shock and awe. The basic idea is almost elegant  in its simplicity. You don't grind down an enemy slowly. You don't fight a long war of attrition.  Instead, you hit them so hard and so fast in the first hours of a conflict that the entire system  collapses. You destroy the command centers. You take out the communication networks. You eliminate  the leadership. And once the head is gone, the body doesn't know what to do. The soldiers panic.  The army loses coordination. The government falls apart. And the war ends almost before the average  person even realizes it started. And for a while, this approach seemed to work beautifully.  Think about the Gulf War in 1991. Think about the invasion of Iraq in 2003. In both cases,  American forces launched massive air campaigns. Cruise missiles flew through the night sky and hit  government buildings with extraordinary precision. Radar installations were destroyed. Communication  systems went dark. And within days, the opposing government had effectively collapsed. People  watched it happen on television and concluded that this was the future of warfare. that modern  conflicts could be resolved quickly, decisively, and with relatively limited casualties, at least  on the American side, if you simply hit the enemy with enough technological force fast enough. But  here is where things start to get complicated. And this is the part of the conversation that  military planners are genuinely wrestling with right now. The world has changed. The enemies  that America and other major powers face today are not organized the same way that Iraq was  organized in 1991 or 2003. And because of that, the assumptions built into shock and all may  no longer hold up. Here's what I mean. Shock and works on a specific assumption. It assumes  that military and political power is centralized. It assumes there is a capital city, a central  command, a military headquarters, a leadership structure that can be identified, targeted, and  destroyed. Destroy the center and the whole system falls. That logic makes sense if your enemy is  built like a traditional nation state with a pyramid structure of command. But what happens  when your enemy is not built that way? What happens when military units operate independently  with their own objectives and their own supply networks and their own communication systems? What  happens when even if you kill the top leadership, the units on the ground already know what they are  supposed to do and just keep doing it? Suddenly, destroying the capital city doesn't end the  war. It just changes the battlefield. The conflict becomes more chaotic, more distributed,  and in many ways harder to control. This is the concept of decentralized command and it has  become a defining feature of how many modern military organizations operate. They are built  specifically to survive the kind of attack that shock and awe is designed to deliver. You can cut  off the head and the body keeps moving. Now add to this something called asymmetrical warfare and  the picture gets even more complex. Asymmetrical warfare is when two sides in a conflict are not  fighting the same kind of war. One side might have aircraft carriers, stealth bombers, satellite  networks, and precision guided munitions that cost millions of dollars each. The other side  might have none of those things. But instead of trying to match that firepower directly, the  weaker side fights completely differently. Let me give you a very concrete example because this  is not theoretical anymore. This is happening right now. Imagine one side of a conflict is  using small drones. Simple, relatively cheap, mass-produced drones. Some of them cost around  $40 or $50,000 each. These drones can fly hundreds of miles. They can carry explosive payloads.  They can strike power plants, oil refineries, military bases, port infrastructure. They are  easy to build in large numbers. They are easy to transport and hide. They can be launched from  pickup trucks or from the backs of buildings. Now, think about how the technologically superior side  defends against this. They have advanced missile defense systems. sophisticated radar networks  that track incoming objects and launch interceptor missiles to destroy them before they hit their  targets. These systems are genuinely remarkable pieces of technology. But here is the problem.  Each interceptor missile might cost a million dollars. Some of them cost significantly more than  that. And sometimes one missile is not enough to take down a drone. You might need to fire two  or three to guarantee a kill. So do the math. A $50,000 drone gets shot down by $2 million  worth of interceptor missiles. And if the other side launches 100 drones in a single night, you  will burning through $200 million of defense budget in a few hours to stop weapons that cost $5  million to produce. That is the economic logic of asymmetrical warfare. The weaker side forces the  stronger side to spend itself into exhaustion. And the stronger side with all its technological  brilliance can find itself trapped in a situation where winning each individual battle becomes  financially unsustainable. And this problem is only going to get worse as technology continues  to evolve. Because in the past, building serious military hardware required massive industrial  capacity. You needed huge factories, enormous supply chains, highly specialized labor. Only  the biggest and wealthiest nations could produce advanced weapons. But that is changing. Drones  can be assembled in relatively modest facilities. Electronic components are commercially available  on the global market. Software can be written by small teams of engineers working in ordinary  office buildings. The industrial barrier to entry for certain kinds of military capability is  dropping. And what this means is that the monopoly on sophisticated firepower that major powers like  the United States have relied on is eroding. Not disappearing, but eroding. And when that advantage  shrinks even slightly, the assumptions built into doctrines like shock and awe start to look more  fragile. There's another layer to this that often gets underestimated, and that is the role of  ideology and belief. Some armies fight because they are paid to. Some soldiers serve because it  is a profession. But in certain conflicts, people fight because they believe they are defending  something that matters more than their own survival, their religion, their homeland, their  identity, their community. And when a conflict has that kind of meaning for the people fighting it,  the math of traditional military strategy starts to break down. Destroying a leadership structure  does not end a war. When every individual fighter sees themselves as a leader in their own right,  bombing infrastructure does not produce surrender. When the people on the ground interpret suffering  as confirmation that they are on the right side of a righteous struggle in these situations, the  strategy of shock and awe can actually backfire. It can strengthen resolve. It can create martyrs.  It can deepen the conflict rather than ending it. Now, pull all of this together and you start to  understand why people are genuinely worried about what a major conflict would look like today. not  a conflict against a small disorganized opponent, but a serious confrontation between powers that  have thought carefully about how to counter American military doctrine. What would happen if  shock and all was deployed and it simply did not produce the expected collapse? What comes next?  Because the doctrine was never really designed with a plan B in mind. It was built on the  assumption that overwhelming force in the opening hours would be decisive. If that assumption  proves wrong, a war that was supposed to end in days could drag on for years and the economic  consequences of that kind of prolonged conflict would not stay contained to the battlefield. We  live in a deeply interconnected global economy. Energy markets, financial markets, supply chains,  all of it is woven together in ways that make largecale disruption extremely dangerous. Oil  prices spike, trade routes get blocked, financial markets go into freefall. A regional military  conflict becomes a global economic crisis almost overnight. This is why military doctrine matters  for people who have no interest in military affairs because doctrine shapes how leaders think  about whether and when to use force. It shapes the assumptions they bring to crisis situations.  And if those assumptions are based on a model of warfare that no longer reflects reality, leaders  may make decisions that produce outcomes nobody intended and nobody wanted. The doctrines that  dominate military thinking today were largely developed in a world that no longer exists. They  were products of the Cold War era shaped by Cold War assumptions refined through conflicts that  may not be representative of what comes next. The question of whether they have adapted  fast enough to the new realities of drones, decentralized command, asymmetrical tactics, and  ideologically motivated fighters is one that no military establishment in the world can answer  with complete confidence. So when you hear people talking about the possibility of a major conflict  in the Middle East or in Asia, understand that underneath all the news coverage and political  commentary, there is a deeper and more unsettling question being asked. Not just whether a war might  happen, but whether the strategies that powerful nations believe will win that war are actually  going to work when tested against real opponents in the real world. Because if shock and awe is a  doctrine of the past dressed up as a strategy for the future, then the next great conflict could  reveal that in the most costly way imaginable.
